item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So … this thing is attracted to top tens ? I need this in my life, can we point it at Buzzfeed ? :D

Upvoted.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP mentions the Chaos Insurgency, which has a hub. I propose crosslinking to that. It also mentions SCiPNET, which is a term that I believe originated in SCP-2317, so I propose linking that too.

Editing this:

believed to have originated within the Chaos Insurgency, that inhabits the Foundation SCiPNET Database.

To this:

believed to have originated within the Chaos Insurgency, that inhabits the Foundation SCiPNET Database.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the CI didnt create this virus, but only found and released it, maybe it knows some things about these SCPs…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing happens really and the anomaly is kinda boring that is only held together by the meta aspect of it. Most of the article could be summed up too like the Special Containment Procedures (placement of it just feels too unnecessary btw) since a lot of it feels extraneous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, putting the CP and the end confused me because I thought it was part of the anomaly's effect and I was left wondering why you didn't bother shuffling it more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely agree. I'm considering self-deleting this one, but I'm not sure yet. I definitely don't feel like it's quite up to my own standards.

Edit: As to your question, the reason why I put the containment procedures at the end is because the meta thing is the closest thing to a climax that this has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I managed to give this a re-read in recent times, but I can't understand why you chose to put the procedures at the end of the article.

Ultimately, I think the article's flaw lies in it stopping at 'this is why the mainlist is like that'. It covers the meta aspect, while leaving other aspects hollow. Thus, while it is a quick 'aw' at first, it gives way for a mediocre work that somehow dares not to go further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you are still looking at editing this and doing adjustments, maybe have the picture as the results of an attempt to outwit the SCP (IE an attempt to just implement the names), and an addition of the sometimes quirky names being part of the protocol (thus adding further randomness and further lower the SSO score)?

Overall, I love this idea, it's great. +1

Hope my comment isn't too out of line for a lurker who occasionally pops in

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Moral of the story, I think, is that I shouldn't write articles based on containment. Both of my lowest rated pages started like that.

I need to make like my best two, and focus on the description and addenda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it as is.
The Foundation has an anomaly in its network that could destroy the howl world if it hits the wrong file and every Keter could be that file.

It is like a gigantic Russian roulette, just with the whole world is on the line.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a clever answer to the mainlist lack-of-order, which I like, but ultimately it's just not very interesting. There's no story and no characters. It's just the minimum possible anomaly that explains the mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this idea, though you really can do so much more with it. As it is now it feels like the concept is an excuse for an article otherwise bereft of interest, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Maybe do what Communism will win said and give reasons for the nicknames. Perhaps, it didn't only delete data, but also released some of it, and interchanged scp designations with different objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly I agree with you, but I figured out pretty quickly while writing it that the concept couldn't support much of a story on its own.

So I just had to hope it would work on its own merits. It hasn't been deleted yet, so that's something I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As usual, I did enjoy this, but, for such a largely important concept here, I feel like there should be some more narrative. Not to say this wasn't well done, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We have D-class writing these lists all the time, and then we just file them away."
"What if we put them on a web page and added ads…"

And thus the listicle was born. So next time you're on Buzzfeed, click a couple ads and throw a little advertising revenue the Foundation's way. It's a small thing you can do to help prevent containment breaches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough - I got some of that in crit, I just didn't know how to fix it.

Sorry you didn't care for it, but I'm not sure I can fix what you say is wrong with it without completely rewriting it.

Thanks for the response though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I've already replaced it with your image, and I'm gonna fix the numbered list here in a sec.

And, uh, yeah. I didn't think of that, and at this point I'm not sure I'll do anything about it just because the names are the only reason the picture would tell the reader something was up. That could be fun though - I might think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I know what you're doing, but it just coalesces into nonsense for me. Things happen and that's why the mainlist is how it is, etc. etc., virus and cognitohazard, but it bores me because it just comes together into what feels like nothing.

Props for making it short, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fun. A few notes:

  • Nitpick, but the recovered CI email should have a # numbered list, rather than * bullets.
  • The pic is pretty blurry and unfun to look at. I've recreated it at a native 300px here, so it should consistently look crisp. You should also localize the images when you get the chance.
  • I think it would be even better if you could find a way to explain why SCPs are given idiosyncratic names as well. That could be part of the same anomaly, but the picture implies that the Foundation was doing that already. Hard to excuse that as an out-of-universe quirk when we've already given an in-universe explanation for one of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As you've probably figured out, this is an attempt to create an in-universe explanation for the mechanics of the mainlist - namely, the whole no-particular-order thing.

I'm not sure how well it will do, partly because of the format screw (I've never tried one before). Let me know what you think!

Edit: Apparently I've written something super divisive. Upvote/downvote ratio is almost exactly 50%.
